questionText: A local government orally agreed with a towing company that the company would provide towing and storage services under a government towing program for four years, and the government would pay the company under the agreed schedule. Both sides understood from the start that the arrangement was meant to last for the full four-year program, not on an at-will basis. The towing company began performance, but before the first year ended, the program was shut down and the government stopped using the company. No signed writing was exchanged. Is the agreement enforceable? Analyze.

responses.responseText:
Probably not as an enforceable four-year contract.

The main issue is the Statute of Frauds. An agreement must generally be in a signed writing if, by its terms, it cannot be fully performed within one year from the time it is made. Here, the parties orally agreed to a four-year arrangement, and the facts say both sides understood from the start that it was to last for the full four-year program, not be terminable at will. That means full performance was not possible within one year under the contract’s intended terms. So the agreement falls within the one-year provision of the Statute of Frauds.

Because no signed writing was exchanged, the four-year promise is ordinarily unenforceable.

The towing company’s beginning performance does not usually cure that problem. Part performance is not, in most jurisdictions, an exception to the one-year provision of the Statute of Frauds. If the company had fully performed its side, some courts would allow enforcement of the other side’s payment obligation, but that did not happen here—the program ended before even the first year was complete.

The fact that the government shut down the program before a year passed does not make the agreement performable within one year. The test is whether the contract, as made, could be fully performed within a year—not whether it might be terminated, breached, or excused within a year. A premature shutdown is not the same as full contractual performance.

There is also a second, independent problem: contracts with local governments are often enforceable only if statutory or ordinance-based formalities are followed, such as approval by the proper body and execution in writing by authorized officials. Governments are often not bound by informal oral arrangements in the same way private parties are. So even apart from the general Statute of Frauds, the oral agreement may fail because municipal contracting rules typically require formal authorization and a written contract.

What the towing company may still have is a restitution or quantum meruit claim for the value of services actually rendered before the shutdown. In other words, even if the four-year contract itself is unenforceable, the company may recover payment for towing and storage services it already provided and the government accepted. But that is different from enforcing the full four-year bargain or recovering lost profits for the remaining term.

So the best analysis is:

1. The oral four-year agreement is within the Statute of Frauds.
2. No signed writing exists.
3. Partial performance before the first year ended does not ordinarily make the four-year promise enforceable.
4. Government-contract formalities make enforcement even less likely.
5. The company may be limited to recovery for the reasonable value of services already performed, not expectancy damages for the remaining years.

Bottom line: the oral agreement is likely not enforceable as a binding four-year contract.

The basic contract problem is the statute of frauds. An agreement that, by its terms, cannot be fully performed within one year must generally be evidenced by a writing signed by the party to be charged. Here, both sides understood from the outset that the towing arrangement was to run for the full four-year government program. That makes this a fixed-term, four-year services contract, not an at-will relationship. Because a four-year contract cannot be fully performed within one year, it falls within the one-year provision of the statute of frauds.

No signed writing was exchanged, so the oral agreement is ordinarily unenforceable as to the unperformed balance of the four-year term. The fact that the towing company began performance does not usually cure that defect for a services contract under the one-year clause. Part performance may support payment for what was actually done, but it generally does not make the entire oral four-year promise enforceable. Likewise, the fact that the program was shut down before the first year ended does not help. Early shutdown is not “performance” of the four-year bargain; it is at most termination, frustration, or breach. The statute of frauds asks whether the contract could be fully performed within a year according to its terms, and this one could not.

There is also a separate, often stronger, municipal-contracting issue. Local governments usually may be bound only in the manner authorized by statute, ordinance, charter, or procurement rules. Those rules commonly require written contracts, approvals, appropriations, bidding or program procedures, and execution by designated officials. If those formalities were required here and were not followed, the oral agreement may be unenforceable even apart from the statute of frauds. In public-contract cases, courts are often especially reluctant to enforce oral promises against a municipality based on apparent authority, estoppel, or informal understandings.

So the likely result is:

1. The towing company cannot enforce the alleged promise that it would remain the provider for the entire four-year program and recover expectation damages for the lost remainder of the term.
2. The company can likely recover for services actually rendered and accepted before shutdown, under the agreed rates if provable, or at least in quantum meruit/unjust enrichment for the reasonable value of towing and storage actually provided.
3. Any attempt to use promissory estoppel to enforce the full four-year oral commitment is unlikely to succeed, especially against a local government, unless the jurisdiction is unusually receptive and the officials had actual authority.

In short: the oral agreement is very likely unenforceable as a four-year contract, both because it falls within the statute of frauds and because government contracts often require formal written authorization. The towing company’s best claim is for payment for work already performed, not for the remaining years of the program.

Because no signed writing was exchanged, the towing company ordinarily cannot enforce the oral agreement for the unperformed balance of the four-year term. In other words, it likely cannot recover expectation damages for the lost remaining years.

So the best analysis is:

1. The oral four-year agreement is within the statute of frauds and, absent a signed writing, is unenforceable as a contract for the remaining term.
2. The shutdown before one year does not avoid that result, because the contract was not capable of full performance within one year when made.
3. The towing company may still recover for services already performed and accepted, but usually not for profits or payments due over the rest of the four-year program.
4. If local-government contracting law required a written, formally approved agreement, that independently defeats enforcement and may limit even quasi-contract remedies, depending on the jurisdiction.

Bottom line: the four-year oral agreement is likely not enforceable against the local government as a binding commitment for the full term, though the company may have a claim for compensation for work already performed.

# Enforceability of the Oral Four-Year Towing Agreement

## The Central Issue: The Statute of Frauds

The threshold question is whether this oral agreement falls within the Statute of Frauds, which requires certain categories of contracts to be evidenced by a writing signed by the party to be charged. The most relevant provision here is the "one-year rule": an agreement that by its terms cannot be performed within one year from the date of its making must be in writing.

**Application of the One-Year Rule.** The agreement contemplated a four-year term of performance. Because both parties understood the arrangement was meant to last the full four years—and not on an at-will or terminable-at-any-time basis—the contract by its terms could not be fully performed within one year from the date it was made. This squarely places the agreement within the Statute of Frauds. The absence of any signed writing means the agreement is, on its face, unenforceable under the traditional Statute of Frauds analysis.

Courts construe the one-year provision narrowly: it applies only to contracts that, by their terms, *cannot possibly* be performed within one year. Here, the facts specify that both sides understood the arrangement was meant to last the full four-year program and was not at-will. There is no plausible reading of the agreement under which full performance could occur within one year. Unlike a contract for lifetime services (which could theoretically be completed within a year by the promisor's death), a fixed four-year term is definitionally incapable of being performed within one year.

## Potential Avenues Around the Statute of Frauds

### 1. Part Performance

The towing company began performance—it actually provided towing and storage services under the program. The doctrine of part performance is most robustly recognized in the context of real property transactions, where courts routinely allow it to take an oral agreement outside the Statute of Frauds when the performing party has changed position in reliance on the contract. In the context of service contracts falling under the one-year rule, however, courts are split and generally more reluctant to apply part performance as a complete exception. Many jurisdictions hold that mere commencement of performance on a service contract does not remove the one-year bar.

That said, some courts have recognized that substantial partial performance, combined with the other party's acceptance of the benefits of that performance, can be sufficient to overcome the Statute of Frauds defense, particularly where the equities strongly favor enforcement.

### 2. Promissory Estoppel / Equitable Estoppel

Under the Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 139, a promise that the promisor should reasonably expect to induce action or forbearance on the part of the promisee, and that does induce such action or forbearance, is enforceable notwithstanding the Statute of Frauds if injustice can be avoided only by enforcement. The towing company likely undertook expenditures—acquiring or dedicating tow trucks, hiring staff, forgoing other business opportunities—in reliance on the four-year commitment. If the company can demonstrate reasonable, foreseeable, and detrimental reliance, a court applying § 139 could enforce the promise to the extent necessary to prevent injustice.

However, the application of promissory estoppel to defeat the Statute of Frauds remains controversial. Some jurisdictions apply it freely; others hold that the Statute of Frauds cannot be circumvented by estoppel at all, or limit it to extraordinary circumstances.

### 3. Quantum Meruit / Unjust Enrichment (Quasi-Contractual Recovery)

Even if the oral agreement is deemed unenforceable as a contract, the towing company has a strong claim for restitutionary recovery. The government received the benefit of towing and storage services actually rendered. Allowing the government to retain those benefits without paying would constitute unjust enrichment. The company would be entitled to recover the reasonable value of services actually provided, though this recovery would be limited to work already performed and would not include expectation damages (i.e., lost profits for the remaining years of the four-year term).

### 4. The Government Entity Dimension

An additional wrinkle is that the agreement is with a local government. Many jurisdictions impose statutory requirements on government contracts beyond the general Statute of Frauds—including competitive bidding requirements, authorization by the governing body, and mandatory written memorialization. Under these rules, an oral agreement with a government entity may be void or voidable regardless of the Statute of Frauds analysis, on the independent ground that the government lacked authority to bind itself without following prescribed procedures. This is sometimes called the "ultra vires" doctrine as applied to municipal contracts. If such rules apply, even equitable doctrines like estoppel may be unavailable, because courts are reluctant to estop government entities from asserting procedural defenses designed to protect the public fisc.

However, some courts have held that where a municipality has actually received and accepted the benefits of services, it cannot invoke its own failure to follow contracting procedures to avoid paying for what it received. This is essentially a restitution-based argument rather than a contract-enforcement argument.

## Likely Outcome

**The oral agreement is most likely unenforceable as a contract.** The four-year term places it squarely within the Statute of Frauds' one-year provision, and no signed writing exists. The part performance doctrine, while potentially applicable in some jurisdictions, is an uncertain basis for enforcement of a service contract under the one-year rule. Promissory estoppel under § 139 offers a possible path but is jurisdiction-dependent and often limited in scope. The government-contracting dimension adds a further layer of difficulty.

**The towing company's strongest claim is for quantum meruit recovery** for the reasonable value of services actually rendered before the program was shut down. This does not require enforcing the oral agreement itself but instead rests on the independent equitable principle that the government should not retain the benefit of services without paying for them.

**The company's weakest position is in seeking expectation damages**—that is, compensation for the profits it would have earned over the remaining three-plus years of the four-year term. Recovering such forward-looking damages would require enforcing the oral contract itself, which the Statute of Frauds almost certainly bars.

In sum, the oral agreement fails the Statute of Frauds, and the most the towing company can realistically recover is restitution for services already performed, not the benefit of the full four-year bargain.

# Enforceability of the Oral Four-Year Towing Services Agreement

## The Statute of Frauds Problem

The central obstacle to enforceability is the Statute of Frauds. Under the traditional rule—codified in virtually every American jurisdiction—a contract that by its terms cannot be performed within one year from the date of its making must be evidenced by a writing signed by the party to be charged. An oral agreement explicitly contemplated by both parties to last four years falls squarely within this provision. Because no signed writing was exchanged, the agreement is prima facie unenforceable under the one-year clause of the Statute of Frauds.

The critical question under the one-year rule is not whether the contract *will* be performed within a year, but whether it *could possibly* be fully performed within one year from the date of making. Here, both sides understood the arrangement was meant to last the full four-year program. Unlike an indefinite-term agreement—which courts routinely hold falls outside the Statute because it *could* theoretically be completed within a year (for example, by full performance, death, or some contingency)—this agreement was specifically tied to a defined four-year program. A court would almost certainly conclude that full performance within one year was not possible, bringing the agreement within the Statute.

## Partial Performance as an Exception

# Enforceability of the Oral Four-Year Towing Agreement

## The Core Problem: The Statute of Frauds

The central issue is whether this oral agreement falls within the **Statute of Frauds**, which requires certain contracts to be evidenced by a signed writing to be enforceable. Under virtually every jurisdiction's version of the statute (derived from the original English statute and codified in state law), an agreement that **by its terms cannot be performed within one year from the date of its making** must be in writing and signed by the party to be charged.

Here, the agreement was expressly understood to last for **four years**. Because the contract by its terms contemplates performance extending well beyond one year, it squarely falls within the one-year provision of the Statute of Frauds. An oral agreement of this nature is **presumptively unenforceable** under the traditional rule.

## Analyzing Possible Exceptions and Counterarguments

### 1. The "Possibility of Performance Within One Year" Doctrine

Many courts apply the Statute of Frauds narrowly, holding that only contracts that are **impossible** to perform within one year fall within the statute. If there is any conceivable way the contract could be fully performed within a year—even if unlikely—some courts hold the statute does not apply. For example, a contract to perform services "for life" is often held outside the statute because the promisor could die within a year, thereby fully performing.

Here, however, this exception is unlikely to help the towing company. The parties expressly understood the arrangement was to last the **full four years**. Unlike a lifetime contract, there is no contingency by which full performance could occur within one year. The company was to provide towing services over the entire four-year program period, and the government was to pay according to a schedule presumably extending across that term. Full performance within one year is not merely unlikely—it is inconsistent with the express understanding of the parties. Most courts would hold this agreement falls within the statute.

### 2. Part Performance

The towing company did begin performing under the agreement, which raises the **part performance** doctrine. However, part performance as an exception to the Statute of Frauds operates differently depending on the type of contract:

- **For contracts involving the sale of land**, part performance (such as taking possession, making improvements, or paying consideration) is a well-recognized equitable exception.
- **For contracts falling under the one-year provision**, courts are **far more reluctant** to apply a part performance exception. The majority rule is that **mere partial performance does not take a service contract out of the one-year provision** of the Statute of Frauds. The Restatement (Second) of Contracts §130 comment a and many jurisdictions confirm that partial performance alone is insufficient to overcome the one-year rule, though full performance by one side may be treated differently.

If the towing company had **fully performed** its obligations and only payment remained, many courts would enforce the oral agreement on the theory that the purpose of the statute is satisfied once one party has completely performed. But here, the company had not completed performance—it performed for less than one year of a four-year term before the program was shut down. Partial performance standing alone will generally not remove the contract from the statute's reach under the one-year provision.

### 3. Promissory Estoppel / Equitable Estoppel

Under **Restatement (Second) of Contracts §139**, a promise that the promisor should reasonably expect to induce action or forbearance, and which does induce such action or forbearance, may be enforceable notwithstanding the Statute of Frauds if **injustice can be avoided only by enforcement of the promise**. Courts consider factors including:

- The reasonableness of the promisee's reliance
- Whether the reliance was foreseeable
- The extent and nature of the action taken in reliance
- The availability of other remedies

The towing company may argue that it **reasonably relied** on the government's oral promise by investing in equipment, staffing, and infrastructure to perform the four-year contract. If the company incurred substantial costs in reliance, a court applying §139 might enforce the promise or at least award reliance damages. However, courts are **divided** on the availability of promissory estoppel to circumvent the Statute of Frauds, and many jurisdictions are cautious about using it against government entities, particularly where public contracting rules exist.

### 4. Government Contracting Requirements

Beyond the general Statute of Frauds, an additional layer of difficulty exists: **most jurisdictions impose statutory requirements on government contracts** that go beyond the common-law Statute of Frauds. Many states and municipalities require that:

- Government contracts above a certain value or duration be **in writing**
- Contracts be **approved by the governing body** (e.g., city council, county board)
- Contracts comply with **competitive bidding** or procurement statutes
- Contracts be signed by an **authorized official**

These requirements are not merely evidentiary (like the Statute of Frauds) but are often treated as **conditions on the government's power to contract**. An oral agreement that fails to comply with these statutory prerequisites may be **void ab initio**, not merely voidable. Courts have frequently held that parties dealing with the government are **charged with knowledge** of these requirements, and that unauthorized or informal agreements cannot bind the public treasury. This is sometimes called the **ultra vires** doctrine—an agreement made outside the government's authorized contracting procedures is not merely unenforceable but was never validly formed.

If such statutory requirements apply here, the towing company faces an even steeper obstacle than the common-law Statute of Frauds, because equitable doctrines like estoppel are **generally unavailable against the government** when the underlying contract was made in violation of mandatory public contracting statutes. The rationale is that public funds must be protected, and private parties cannot circumvent democratic controls on government spending through informal arrangements.

### 5. Quasi-Contract / Unjust Enrichment

Even if the oral agreement is unenforceable, the towing company likely has a viable claim for **restitution** or **quantum meruit**. The government received the benefit of towing and storage services during the period the company performed. Allowing the government to retain that benefit without compensation would constitute unjust enrichment. Courts routinely permit recovery in quasi-contract even when the underlying agreement is unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds or government contracting statutes, because the claim is not based on enforcing the contract but on preventing unjust retention of benefits.

The measure of recovery would be the **reasonable value of services rendered**, which may or may not correspond to the contract rate the parties orally agreed upon.

## Conclusion

The oral four-year agreement is almost certainly **unenforceable as a contract**. It falls within the one-year provision of the Statute of Frauds because it cannot, by its terms, be performed within one year. Partial performance by the towing company does not remove it from the statute under the majority rule applicable to the one-year provision. Promissory estoppel under §139 offers a possible but uncertain path, and its application against a government entity is particularly disfavored. Moreover, government contracting statutes likely impose independent writing and approval requirements that further bar enforcement, and these requirements are typically immune to equitable override.

The towing company's most reliable remedy is a **quasi-contractual claim for the reasonable value of services actually rendered** during the period of performance. This avoids the Statute of Frauds problem entirely and addresses the fundamental unfairness of the government receiving services without paying for them. However, the company cannot recover **expectation damages** (i.e., lost profits for the remaining years of the four-year term) because there is no enforceable contract on which to base such a claim.

The towing company did begin performance and rendered services before the program was terminated. The partial performance doctrine, however, operates differently in the context of the one-year provision than it does under the real property provision of the Statute of Frauds.

# Enforceability of the Oral Four-Year Towing Agreement

## The Statute of Frauds Problem

The threshold issue is whether this oral agreement falls within the Statute of Frauds. Under the traditional one-year provision found in virtually every jurisdiction's version of the Statute of Frauds, a contract that by its terms cannot be performed within one year from the date of its making must be evidenced by a writing signed by the party to be charged. Here, the parties expressly contemplated a four-year arrangement—well beyond one year. Because the agreement by its terms could not be fully performed within one year, it squarely falls within the Statute of Frauds.

The key question under the one-year provision is not whether the contract *might* end within a year (through breach, termination, or cancellation), but whether full performance on both sides *could* be completed within one year. Courts have consistently held that a contract for a definite term exceeding one year—here, four years—cannot by its terms be performed within one year. The fact that the program was actually shut down before the first year ended is irrelevant to the analysis; the Statute looks to what the parties agreed at the outset, not what actually transpired.

This means the oral agreement is, on its face, unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds absent some exception.

## Potential Exceptions and Avenues to Enforcement

### Part Performance

The most significant potential exception is part performance. The towing company actually began rendering towing and storage services under the program. Under general contract principles, part performance can take an oral agreement outside the Statute of Frauds, but the scope of this doctrine varies by jurisdiction and by subject matter.

In the context of service contracts (as opposed to real property conveyances, where part performance is more broadly recognized as an exception), many courts are reluctant to allow part performance alone to overcome the one-year provision of the Statute. The traditional rule is that part performance does not remove a service contract from the one-year provision of the Statute of Frauds. The Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 130 comment a acknowledges the strictness of this rule. Under this traditional approach, the towing company's commencement of performance would not, by itself, make the four-year oral agreement enforceable.

However, some courts and the modern trend recognize that where one party has substantially performed or the other party has accepted the benefits of performance, enforcing the Statute of Frauds defense would work an injustice. In those jurisdictions, part performance coupled with other circumstances may suffice.

### Promissory Estoppel / Equitable Estoppel

Under Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 139, a promise that the promisor should reasonably expect to induce action or forbearance, and that does induce such action or forbearance, is enforceable notwithstanding the Statute of Frauds if injustice can be avoided only by enforcement. The towing company could argue:

1. The government made a clear oral promise of a four-year arrangement.
2. The government reasonably expected the company to rely on that promise by investing in equipment, staffing, and operational capacity to serve the program.
3. The company did in fact rely, commencing performance and potentially foregoing other business opportunities.
4. Injustice can be avoided only by enforcing the promise (or at minimum awarding reliance damages).

This is a strong argument, particularly if the company made substantial investments or changed its position in reliance on the four-year term. Courts applying § 139 weigh the reasonableness and foreseeability of the reliance, the extent to which the evidentiary and cautionary purposes of the Statute are satisfied by other means (here, the government's own conduct in using the company's services confirms the existence and terms of the agreement), and the degree of hardship.

### Quasi-Contract / Unjust Enrichment

Even if the oral agreement is unenforceable, the towing company can recover in restitution for the reasonable value of services actually rendered. The government received towing and storage services, and it would be unjust to allow the government to retain those benefits without paying. This claim does not enforce the contract itself but provides compensation for work done. However, restitution would only cover services already performed; it would not give the company the benefit of its bargain for the remaining years of the four-year term.

### The Government as a Party: Additional Complications

There is a separate issue regarding whether the government can enter into binding contracts orally at all. Many jurisdictions impose additional formality requirements on government contracts—statutory provisions requiring competitive bidding, written contracts, authorization by the governing body, or compliance with procurement codes. If the local government's own charter or state law requires government contracts to be in writing or to follow specific procurement procedures, the oral agreement may be unenforceable on independent grounds beyond the general Statute of Frauds. This is a significant practical barrier. Courts have frequently refused to enforce informal government contracts where statutory procedures were not followed, reasoning that public funds require procedural safeguards.

That said, even where the contract itself is unenforceable, courts generally allow quantum meruit recovery against the government for services actually received, to prevent unjust enrichment of the public entity.

## Analysis of Likely Outcome

The oral agreement is most likely **unenforceable as a four-year contract**. It falls within the Statute of Frauds' one-year provision, no signed writing exists, and the traditional rule does not recognize part performance as an exception to the one-year rule for service contracts. The additional governmental formality requirements in most jurisdictions would independently bar enforcement.

However, the towing company has viable claims. First, it can recover in quantum meruit for the reasonable value of all towing and storage services actually performed before the program was shut down. Second, under § 139 of the Restatement, a court in a jurisdiction following the modern approach might enforce the promise to the extent necessary to prevent injustice—potentially awarding reliance damages (the company's out-of-pocket losses incurred in reliance on the four-year commitment) even if not full expectation damages. Third, if the company can show particularly egregious reliance—such as purchasing specialized equipment or turning down other contracts—a court might estop the government from raising the Statute of Frauds defense entirely, though this is the least likely outcome, especially given that the company (as a commercial entity) should have insisted on a written contract.

## Conclusion

The four-year oral agreement is almost certainly unenforceable as written under the Statute of Frauds and likely under additional government contracting formality requirements. The company's strongest path to recovery is quantum meruit for services already rendered and, in a sympathetic jurisdiction, promissory estoppel or § 139 reliance damages for provable losses stemming from reasonable reliance on the government's four-year commitment. The agreement itself, however—particularly the right to perform and be paid for the remaining years—is not enforceable absent a signed writing.

Probably not, at least not as an enforceable four-year contract.

The core problem is the statute of frauds. At common law, an agreement that by its terms cannot be fully performed within one year must be evidenced by a writing signed by the party to be charged. This was an oral agreement for a fixed four-year term, and both sides understood from the outset that it was to last the full four years, not be terminable at will. That means full performance was not possible within one year. So the agreement falls squarely within the one-year provision of the statute of frauds.

The fact that the program was shut down before the first year ended does not make the contract performable within one year. The legal test is whether the contract, when made, could be fully performed within a year according to its terms. Here, performance required four years of towing and storage services and government payment over that period. Early shutdown is termination or excuse, not completion of the promised four-year performance.

Because no signed writing was exchanged, the towing company ordinarily cannot enforce the oral agreement for the unperformed balance of the four-year term. In other words, it likely cannot recover expectation damages for the lost remaining years.

The company’s beginning performance does not usually cure the statute-of-frauds problem for a services contract under the one-year provision. Partial performance may support recovery for the value of services already rendered, but it generally does not make the entire oral multi-year service contract enforceable. So the towing company may still be entitled to payment for towing and storage services it actually performed and the government accepted before shutdown, either under the agreed rate if provable, or in quantum meruit for their reasonable value. But that is different from enforcing the oral promise to keep the company for the full four years.

There is an additional and often stronger obstacle because the other party is a local government. Municipal and local government contracts are commonly subject to formal requirements—such as approval by the proper body, compliance with procurement rules, and a written contract. If those requirements were not followed, the contract may be not merely unenforceable under the statute of frauds but void as a public contract. Courts are often especially reluctant to bind a government entity based on oral agreements, even where government officials clearly assented, because parties dealing with government are charged with notice of statutory contracting formalities.

So the best analysis is:

1. The oral four-year agreement is within the statute of frauds and, absent a signed writing, is unenforceable as a contract for the remaining term.
2. The shutdown before one year does not avoid that result, because the contract was not capable of full performance within one year when made.
3. The towing company may still recover for services already performed and accepted, but usually not for profits or payments due over the rest of the four-year program.
4. If local-government contracting law required a written, formally approved agreement, that independently defeats enforcement and may limit even quasi-contract remedies, depending on the jurisdiction.

Bottom line: the four-year oral agreement is likely not enforceable against the local government as a binding commitment for the full term, though the company may have a claim for compensation for work already performed.

The oral agreement between the local government and the towing company is not enforceable primarily because it violates the Statute of Frauds. 

Under the Statute of Frauds, any contract that by its express terms cannot be fully performed within one year from the date it is made must be evidenced by a writing signed by the party against whom enforcement is sought. The facts indicate that the parties agreed to a four-year term for the towing and storage services, and both sides understood the arrangement was to last for the full four years rather than operating on an at-will basis. Because it is physically and legally impossible to complete a four-year service contract within a single year, the agreement falls squarely within the one-year provision of the Statute of Frauds. Since no signed writing was exchanged, the contract is legally unenforceable.

Although the towing company began performance, part performance generally does not remove a multi-year service contract from the Statute of Frauds. While part performance can sometimes create an exception for oral contracts involving the sale of real estate or specially manufactured goods, the majority rule for service contracts is that partial performance does not validate an oral agreement that spans more than one year. The towing company would be entitled to recover the reasonable value of the services it already provided prior to the program's shutdown under an equitable theory such as quantum meruit or unjust enrichment. However, it cannot enforce the remainder of the four-year executory contract or recover expectation damages for the unperformed years.

Furthermore, the agreement suffers from an additional, fatal defect regarding government contracting. Contracts with local governments are subject to strict statutory and municipal formalities. As a matter of public policy, local governments typically can only be bound by written contracts that have been formally approved by the relevant governing body (such as a city council or county commission) and signed by an authorized public official. Oral agreements with a municipality are generally void ab initio (void from the beginning) because they bypass mandatory public transparency, budgeting, and approval procedures. Even if promissory estoppel is raised, courts rarely enforce oral promises against the government unless there is evidence of extreme affirmative misconduct, which is absent here. 

Therefore, due to both the Statute of Frauds and the strict formalities required to bind a local government, the four-year oral agreement is unenforceable.
